cover.tagline: The #1 Performance Running Brand Running on 2018-Era Search
cover.verdict: Functional but dated — 3 critical gaps costing ~$11.5M/year in search-addressable revenue

company_snapshot.name: Brooks Running
company_snapshot.hq: Seattle, WA (3400 Stone Way North STE 500)
company_snapshot.ownership: Private — wholly-owned subsidiary of Berkshire Hathaway Inc. (since 2006)
company_snapshot.revenue_note: ESTIMATE — Brooks does not disclose absolute revenue. Derived from confirmed +16% YoY growth rate applied to trade media estimate.
company_snapshot.market_position: #1 adult performance running footwear brand in U.S. national retail (11+ consecutive quarters through 2024)
company_snapshot.growth_streak: 9 consecutive years of record revenue (13–14% CAGR)
company_snapshot.search_provider: Salesforce Commerce Cloud native SearchServices (Demandware — no external search vendor)
company_snapshot.search_provider_evidence: Network inspection confirmed all search API calls routed to /on/demandware.store/Sites-BrooksRunning-Site/en_US/SearchServices-GetSuggestions — zero calls to Constructor.io or any external provider
company_snapshot.search_vendor_status: NO external search vendor — SFCC native only
company_snapshot.search_vendor_source: Network inspection March 17 2026 — zero calls to external search providers

executives.title: CEO

executives.quote: Most runners start with a digital transaction — a search for a product or a brand.
executives.maps_to: CEO validates search-first purchase journey — Algolia solves the problem he named directly

executives.quote: We feel great about where Brooks is in the market right now, and the momentum is exciting going into 2025.

intelligence_signals.title: 🔴 CRITICAL: Senior Engineer Digital Products — Vacancy Open
intelligence_signals.body: Role posted March 7, 2026 with JD language: 'evaluate emerging technologies, integrate practical AI solutions, vendor RFP processes, third-party integrations, Salesforce Commerce Cloud.' This is the Technical Buyer seat — currently empty. Engage IT leadership NOW before role is filled and new engineer develops vendor preference.
intelligence_signals.relevance: Directly relevant to Algolia search investment decision

intelligence_signals.title: 9th Consecutive Year of Record Revenue — Scaling Pains Imminent
intelligence_signals.body: FY2025: +16% YoY | Q1 2025: +15% | Q3 2025: +19% surge | APLA: +66% | China: +245%. Platform-native SFCC search was designed for a smaller catalog and traffic volume. Growth at this pace creates relevance degradation without AI indexing.
intelligence_signals.relevance: Directly relevant to Algolia search investment decision

intelligence_signals.title: CEO's $4B Revenue Ambition — Search Conversion is a Direct Lever
intelligence_signals.body: Dan Sheridan on record: 'Brooks could become a $4 billion brand.' Current ~$1.6B → $4B = 2.5x growth. At ~$115M search-addressable revenue, a 10% search conversion improvement = $11.5M/year — direct contribution toward the $4B goal.
intelligence_signals.relevance: Directly relevant to Algolia search investment decision

intelligence_signals.title: Lifestyle Footwear Expansion — New SKU Category Requires New Discovery
intelligence_signals.body: Brooks debuted lifestyle footwear at Paris Fashion Week 2025 (Brooks x STAPLE collab). Lifestyle buyers search with different intent than performance runners. Current SFCC native search has no persona-aware faceting — risks poor discovery for new buyer segment.
intelligence_signals.relevance: Directly relevant to Algolia search investment decision

intelligence_signals.title: Run Club 2M Members — Personalization Data Ready, Search Unused
intelligence_signals.body: Brooks Run Club reached 2M members by 2025 (2x growth from 2024). DashLX partnership delivers 23% higher AOV via behavior-triggered personalization for Run Club loyalty. Yet search results are identical for all users — personalization investment stops at loyalty, never reaching discovery.
intelligence_signals.relevance: Directly relevant to Algolia search investment decision

intelligence_signals.title: CEO: 'Most Runners Start With a Digital Search'
intelligence_signals.body: Dan Sheridan (as COO): 'Most runners start with a digital transaction — a search for a product or a brand.' This is a direct executive acknowledgment of the search-first purchase journey — the problem Algolia solves is one the CEO has personally named.
intelligence_signals.relevance: Directly relevant to Algolia search investment decision

intelligence_signals.title: CEO on $4B Brand Vision
intelligence_signals.body: Dan Sheridan, CEO: 'Brooks could become a $4 billion brand.' — Fortune interview, July 2025. Current estimated revenue ~$1.6B. Search conversion at scale is a proven lever toward that ambition.
intelligence_signals.relevance: Directly relevant to Algolia search investment decision

findings.title: Zero NLP: 'Best Shoes for Marathon Training' Redirects to Generic Bestsellers
findings.expected_behavior: Parse natural language intent → filter to cushioned road running shoes suitable for long distance (Glycerin, Ghost, Adrenaline GTS) with attributes: cushioning=high, surface=road, best_for=long_distance
findings.actual_behavior: Redirected to /en_us/featured/best-sellers/ — generic bestsellers page with no relevance to the query. Query completely ignored. Zero NLP parsing.
findings.impact_stat: 72% of shoppers use natural language queries — 0% are served by Brooks' current search
findings.prospect_description: When a runner types 'best shoes for marathon training,' they're signaling high purchase intent. Brooks' SFCC native search sees an unrecognized query and redirects to a generic page — converting a motivated buyer into a confused visitor.
findings.algolia_solution: Algolia NeuralSearch parses semantic intent — 'marathon training' maps to attributes: cushioning=high, surface=road, best_for=long_distance. Returns Ghost, Glycerin, Adrenaline GTS instead of random bestsellers. Works without pre-built synonym lists.

findings.title: No Personalization: Search Results Identical for All Visitors
findings.tested_query: running shoes (after browsing Ghost 16 product page)
findings.expected_behavior: Returning visitor who browsed Ghost 16 should see Ghost family boosted in results, preferred gender/cushioning surfaced higher, recently viewed product accessible
findings.actual_behavior: Search results for 'running shoes' after visiting Ghost 16 PDP are completely identical to a fresh session — 54 products, same sort order, no personalization signal used whatsoever
findings.impact_stat: Personalized experiences drive 80% higher conversion rates vs non-personalized
findings.prospect_description: Brooks has 2M Run Club members with rich behavioral data. Yet when a Run Club member searches 'running shoes,' they see the same 54 results as a first-time visitor. The DashLX investment in loyalty personalization stops at the loyalty layer — never flowing into search discovery.
findings.algolia_solution: Algolia AI Personalization ingests Run Club behavioral signals (runs logged, gear browsed, past purchases) and re-ranks search results per user. A trail runner sees Cascadia first; a neutral-gait road runner sees Ghost first — without any rule configuration.
findings.algolia_case_study_result: +37% conversion rate, +150% sales contribution from search

findings.title: No True Recommendations: PDP Shows Product Variants, Not Cross-Sell
findings.expected_behavior: PDP should show 'Frequently bought together' (socks, insoles, running accessories), 'You may also like' (Glycerin if user prefers soft cushioning), 'Trending' items
findings.actual_behavior: Ghost 16 PDP shows a 'Compare' section displaying the same shoe in different cushioning variants. No cross-sell, no upsell, no complementary products. Zero recommendation engine present.
findings.impact_stat: 35% of Amazon's revenue comes from recommendation engine — PDP is the highest-intent moment
findings.prospect_description: A runner landing on the Ghost 16 PDP is at peak purchase intent. Brooks shows them the same shoe in different cushion levels — not socks, not insoles, not the Glycerin if they prefer plush cushioning. The cross-sell and upsell opportunity is completely absent.
findings.algolia_solution: Algolia Recommend uses collaborative filtering to show 'Frequently bought together' (socks + insoles purchased alongside Ghost 16 by 68% of buyers), 'Similar items' (Glycerin for plush preference), and 'Trending' for social proof.
findings.algolia_case_study_result: +4.5% conversion during first Cyber Weekend post-implementation

findings.title: No Query Suggestions in Empty State — Users Left Without Guidance
findings.expected_behavior: Empty state should show trending searches (e.g., 'Ghost 17,' 'trail shoes,' 'marathon training'), recent searches for returning users, and popular categories
findings.actual_behavior: Empty state shows 'POPULAR PRODUCTS' section with product tiles only. No popular search queries, no trending searches, no recent searches. Users must know exactly what to type.
findings.impact_stat: Searching users convert at 2-3x the rate of non-searching users — empty state is the first missed conversion opportunity
findings.prospect_description: When a visitor clicks the search bar and sees no query suggestions, they face a blank slate. If they can't articulate a query in Brooks' terms (product model names, technical attributes), they abandon. The empty state is the easiest win — and it's completely missed.
findings.algolia_solution: Algolia Query Suggestions auto-generates trending searches from analytics data — updated daily. Empty state becomes a curated discovery surface showing top 8-10 searches, recent queries for logged-in users, and trending categories.

findings.title: No Merchandising Banners in Search — Strategic Queries Redirect Out
findings.expected_behavior: Query 'sale' should show a promotional banner within search results + filtered discounted products. Query 'trail' should show a Cascadia Elite launch banner + trail category results.
findings.actual_behavior: Query 'sale' redirects directly to /en_us/sale/ category page — exits search entirely. Query 'trail' returns 67 products with no promotional banner or featured content.
findings.impact_stat: Merchandising rules drive 15-25% higher click-through on targeted product categories
findings.prospect_description: Brooks cannot inject promotional content for strategic queries. A user searching 'trail' during a Cascadia Elite launch gets zero awareness of the launch — just a product grid. 'Sale' removes the user from the search experience entirely, breaking the discovery flow.
findings.algolia_solution: Algolia Rules inject promotional banners for any query — 'trail' shows Cascadia Elite launch banner above results, 'sale' displays a discount callout while preserving the search experience. Merchandisers configure this in a no-code dashboard without developer involvement.

findings.title: Static Facets Ignore Query Context — Same Filters for All Searches
findings.expected_behavior: Women's-specific query should surface women's sizes first, hide men's size range, show width options (women's-specific fits), potentially surface 'best for' attributes relevant to women's running categories
findings.actual_behavior: 108 products shown. Facet panel is identical to any other search — same Product Gender, Size, Product Type, Best For, Support Type ordering regardless of query. No query-aware facet adjustment.
findings.impact_stat: 43% of shoppers abandon search when facets are irrelevant to their query
findings.prospect_description: Brooks' facets are hardcoded regardless of the search context. A women's query gets the same filter set as a men's query — requiring extra filter clicks to narrow results. For lifestyle expansion buyers (new customer segment), the standard 'Support Type' and 'Drop' facets are meaningless.
findings.algolia_solution: Algolia Dynamic Facets selects and orders facets per query based on click data. Women's shoe query → width options appear first, men's sizes hidden. Trail query → Surface type and stack height facets promoted. No engineering required to reconfigure.

findings.title: No Results State Unclear — Nonsense Queries Return Products Silently
findings.expected_behavior: Explicit 'No results for asdfghjk. Showing popular products instead.' message with clear recovery paths (popular searches, top categories, contact support)
findings.actual_behavior: 20 products returned (Hyperion Elite 5, Hyperion Max 3). No message indicating query was not understood. User has no way to know their search failed — may assume these are the only products Brooks sells.
findings.impact_stat: No-results handling failures cause 68% of users to abandon the site entirely
findings.prospect_description: When a user makes a typo or searches for something Brooks doesn't carry, they silently receive 20 unrelated race shoes. No explanation. No recovery path. The runner may conclude Brooks doesn't carry what they need and leave — when in reality, the search simply failed.
findings.algolia_solution: Algolia no-results UX: explicit message + Query Suggestions showing similar searchable terms + fallback to top categories. Configurable in no-code dashboard. Zero engineering required.

gap_pairs.said_quote: Most runners start with a digital transaction — a search for a product or a brand.
gap_pairs.found_title: Zero NLP: Natural Language Queries Redirect to Generic Bestsellers
gap_pairs.found_evidence: Typed "best shoes for marathon training" — redirected to /en_us/featured/best-sellers/. Zero NLP parsing. Query completely ignored.
gap_pairs.algolia_angle: Algolia NeuralSearch parses semantic intent — "marathon training" → cushioned road shoes (Glycerin, Ghost, Adrenaline GTS). Under Armour saw +35% conversion after identical upgrade.

gap_pairs.found_title: No Personalization: All 2M Run Club Members See Identical Search Results
gap_pairs.found_evidence: Browsed Ghost 16 PDP, then searched "running shoes" — 54 identical results as anonymous visitor. Zero personalization. Run Club membership not surfaced.
gap_pairs.algolia_angle: Algolia AI Personalization uses Run Club behavioral signals (runs logged, gear browsed) to re-rank results per user. Trail runner sees Cascadia first; road runner sees Ghost first.

gap_pairs.said_quote: The runner is evolving, and they want to have more products from brands like Brooks.
gap_pairs.found_title: No Recommendations: PDP Shows Product Variants Only — Zero Cross-Sell
gap_pairs.found_evidence: Ghost 16 Weatherized PDP shows a Compare section with same shoe in different cushion levels. No "You may also like," no "Customers also bought," no complementary products.
gap_pairs.algolia_angle: Algolia Recommend: Frequently bought together (socks, insoles), Similar items (Glycerin for plush preference), Trending items for social proof. Shoe Carnival saw +4.5% conversion Cyber Weekend.

gap_pairs.said_quote: Most runners start with a digital transaction — a search for a product or a brand.
gap_pairs.found_title: Empty State Shows Products Only — No Trending Searches to Guide Users
gap_pairs.found_evidence: Clicked search bar with no input — dropdown shows POPULAR PRODUCTS tiles only. Zero trending search queries. Zero recent searches. Users must know exact product names to search.
gap_pairs.algolia_angle: Algolia Query Suggestions auto-generates trending searches from analytics — updated daily. Empty state becomes a curated discovery surface showing top 10 searches + recent queries for logged-in Run Club members.

gap_pairs.said_quote: We want to assort our brand wherever the greatest retailers are that are focused on running.
gap_pairs.found_title: Static Facets Ignore Query Context — Same Filters for All Searches
gap_pairs.found_evidence: Searched "women's shoes" — 108 products with identical facet set to any other query. Same Product Gender, Size, Support Type ordering whether searching men's, trail, or lifestyle.
gap_pairs.algolia_angle: Algolia Dynamic Facets selects and orders facets per query from click data. Women's shoes query → width options first, men's sizes hidden. Critical for lifestyle expansion buyer persona who doesn't know running jargon.

gap_pairs.said_quote: There is more opportunity than ever as we look at the total addressable market worldwide.
gap_pairs.found_title: No Merchandising Banners — Strategic Queries Cannot Feature Product Launches
gap_pairs.found_evidence: Searched "trail" — 67 products returned with zero promotional banner. Searched "sale" — redirected to /en_us/sale/ category page, exiting the search experience entirely.
gap_pairs.algolia_angle: Algolia Rules inject promotional banners for any query — "trail" shows Cascadia Elite launch banner above results, "sale" displays discount callout while preserving search. Merchandisers configure in no-code dashboard.

gap_pairs.said_quote: The Brooks team continues to execute at the highest level for customers and runners around the world.
gap_pairs.found_title: Silent No-Results State — Nonsense Queries Return 20 Unrelated Products
gap_pairs.found_evidence: Searched "asdfghjk" — 20 products returned (Hyperion Elite 5, Hyperion Max 3). No message indicating query failed. User has no way to know search returned irrelevant results.
gap_pairs.algolia_angle: Algolia no-results UX: explicit message ("No results for X") + Query Suggestions for recovery + fallback to top categories. Configurable in no-code dashboard without engineering.

ae_fields.icp_fit: HIGH — Performance DTC brand on SFCC, no search vendor, $115M search-addressable revenue, CEO publicly acknowledges search starts purchase journey
ae_fields.deal_type: Greenfield — no search vendor to displace
ae_fields.competitive_scenario: OFFENSIVE PLAY — leapfrog all competitors before any deploy AI search
ae_fields.sales_motion: Search Audit + ROI-led discovery → SFCC cartridge demo
ae_fields.recommended_approach: Lead with CEO's own quote ('most runners start with a search') + show NLP gap (marathon training query) + present $11.5M/year moderate ROI scenario
ae_fields.urgency_trigger: Senior Engineer Digital Products VACANCY (posted 2026-03-07) — engage IT leadership before role filled
ae_fields.build_vs_buy: 70% Buy signal — JD uses 'vendor RFP,' 'third-party integrations;' SFCC selection proves buy-for-non-core philosophy
ae_fields.buyer_readiness: MEDIUM — Technology investment culture, but no confirmed active search RFP. Urgency window: hiring signal.
ae_fields.champion_path: Jessica Brooks (Director, eCommerce Technical Operations) → technical buyer via open Senior Engineer role → CFO Tom Ross for budget
discovery_questions: You grew EMEA 22% and China 245% in FY2025. How are you ensuring runners in Germany get results merchandised for their market — not just a US-first catalog?
With ~$115M in search-addressable revenue, a 10% conversion improvement is $11.5M/year. What would your team do with that headroom toward Dan's $4B vision?
You launched lifestyle footwear at Paris Fashion Week. How does your search today differentiate between a runner looking for training shoes vs a sneaker buyer?
You have 2M Run Club members with rich behavioral data. Does your search surface results based on a member's running history — or does everyone see the same 54 products?
You partnered with Salesforce for commerce, Amperity for CDP, DashLX for loyalty. What's your philosophy on search — build in-house or best-in-class partner?

objection_handling.response: SFCC's native SearchServices handles keyword matching. NeuralSearch handles 'best shoes for marathon training' — semantically understands intent, not just keywords. We saw it fail live in your own search bar.
At ~$115M search-addressable revenue, 5% improvement = $5.75M/year. Algolia costs ~$200K. ROI in 13 days. The question isn't budget — it's opportunity cost of waiting.
We have an SFCC Commerce Cloud cartridge. Your new Senior Engineer can integrate in 2-3 weeks. You already deployed Amperity and DashLX — Algolia follows the same pattern.
Algolia builds the analytics dashboard as part of the platform — you get query volume, zero-results rate, conversion per query, and trend data from day one. No separate analytics build needed.

ae_fields.urgency_level: HIGH — technical buyer vacancy open, engage before role filled
ae_fields.challenger_insight: Dan Sheridan has publicly said "most runners start with a digital search" — yet brooksrunning.com's search cannot parse a natural language running query. You are one conversation away from showing him the gap he named.
ae_fields.talk_track_opener: We tested your search for "best shoes for marathon training" — a phrase your CEO used publicly to describe how runners start their journey. Here's what happened.
ae_fields.cta: 30-minute demo: live test of your search vs Algolia NeuralSearch on the exact query your CEO described
ae_fields.primary_contact: Jessica Brooks, Director eCommerce Technical Operations — linkedin.com/in/jessicabrooks1/

methodology.search_provider_verification: Network inspection via Chrome DevTools — confirmed Salesforce Commerce Cloud SearchServices as sole search handler
methodology.scoring_formula: Weighted sum: HIGH severity areas × 2.0, MEDIUM × 1.0, LOW × 0.5. Overall = Σ(Score × Weight) / ΣWeight
methodology.audit_method: Chrome browser testing at 1440x900 (desktop) and 375x667 (mobile). 20 test scenarios. Network inspection via DevTools to confirm search provider.
methodology.disclaimer: Traffic and financial data are estimates from third-party sources (SimilarWeb, Retail Dive, ECDB). Brooks Running does not publicly disclose absolute revenue figures. All financial estimates labeled ESTIMATE.

competitive_synthesis.scenario_detail: No confirmed AI/enterprise search vendor found among any of the top 5 Brooks competitors (HOKA, New Balance, ASICS, On Running, Saucony). All appear to use platform-native or basic search. Brooks can deploy Algolia and leapfrog the entire competitive set before any confirmed competitor acts.
competitive_synthesis.golden_angle_note: No confirmed competitor using Algolia in this vertical. Use adjacent-vertical proof: Shoe Carnival (footwear retail), Under Armour (athletic apparel), Lacoste (lifestyle footwear).

strategic_angles.hook: You grew EMEA 22%, APLA 66%, and China 245% in FY2025. As you enter new markets, can your search handle regional product preferences, language nuances, and geo-specific inventory?

strategic_angles.hook: Your Run Research Lab studies individual motion paths, not generic averages. Your website should do the same — understand each runner's needs, not show everyone the same 54 results.

strategic_angles.hook: You build shoes in-house because no one understands biomechanics like Brooks. But search isn't your core competency — partner with experts like you do with Salesforce Commerce Cloud.

Search relevance is never 'done' — ongoing maintenance burden without AI layer

strategic_angles.label: Build-Quality Technology Without Building It

touches.subject: Help your new Digital Products Engineer hit the ground running with Algolia + SFCC
touches.cta: 15-min call to share the audit findings
touches.timing: Week 1 — before Senior Engineer role is filled
touches.body:
Hi —

Saw the Senior Engineer — Digital Products posting on LinkedIn (job ID 4382335419). Whoever fills that role will inherit three search limitations baked into SFCC native that no amount of engineering will fix: zero natural language understanding, no personalization from browsing history, and no recommendations on the product page.

We ran a quick audit of brooksrunning.com. Two findings stood out:

— Searched 'best shoes for marathon training.' Got generic bestsellers. The query wasn't parsed for intent — cushioning level, surface, or distance. That's a direct revenue miss: a runner who describes exactly what they need leaves with nothing useful.

— Browsed Ghost 16, then searched 'running shoes.' Results were identical to a fresh session. Zero personalization. Ghost 16 didn't move.

Your new engineer won't be able to fix this with SFCC customization — it's a platform capability gap, not a configuration gap. Algolia layers on top of SFCC with a 2-hour SDK install. No replatform.

Happy to share the full audit before the role is filled — takes 15 minutes. Worth it?

touches.channel: Email to Jessica Brooks (eCommerce Technical Operations)
touches.subject: Under Armour got 35% higher conversion from search — we tested Brooks' search and found the same gaps
touches.cta: Review the audit findings at [audit URL]
touches.body:
Jessica —

Two case studies from your vertical.

Shoe Carnival was on SFCC native search — same platform as Brooks. After switching to Algolia, they saw measurable conversion lift from NLP queries and their merchandising team could run search campaigns without engineering tickets.

Under Armour: +35% higher search conversion rate after Algolia deployment. The biggest driver? Natural language queries. 'Best shoes for marathon training' now returns cushioned road shoes filtered by distance — not bestsellers.

We ran the same test on brooksrunning.com. 'Best shoes for marathon training' today: generic bestsellers, query intent ignored. The runner who typed that just left.

The full audit is 10 slides. Happy to walk you through it — 15 minutes max. I'll show you the exact gaps and how each one maps to a specific Algolia capability.

touches.subject: Direct message
touches.cta: Would you be open to a 20-min chat?
touches.body:
Hi —

Quick question about the Lifestyle Footwear Merchandiser role: when you're building out the lifestyle assortment, how are you surfacing those products to buyers who don't already know Brooks as a performance brand?

Reason I ask — we audited brooksrunning.com and found that merchandising banners in search results aren't active. A 'trail' search returns 67 products but no Cascadia launch callout. 'Sale' redirects to a category page instead of showing sale items with a banner in search.

Algolia lets merchandisers inject banners, pin products, and boost SKUs without engineering tickets — the merchandiser owns it directly. For a lifestyle launch that needs discovery, that's the gap worth closing.

Would you be open to a 20-min chat?

touches.subject: 4-scenario demo tailored to Brooks
touches.body:
Demo flow — 4 scenarios, 20 minutes:

1. NLP live: type 'best shoes for marathon training' in Brooks today → generic bestsellers. Same query in Algolia → filters to cushioned road shoes (Glycerin, Ghost, Adrenaline). Runs live on your catalog.

2. Personalization: Browse Ghost 16 for 30 seconds, then search 'running shoes.' In Algolia: Ghost family boosted, preferred gender surfaced. In Brooks today: identical to fresh session.

3. Merchandiser workflow: Launch a Cascadia trail campaign banner on the 'trail' query — no engineering ticket. Under 2 minutes in the Algolia dashboard.

4. PDP recommendations: Add 'Frequently Bought Together' (socks, insoles) and 'You may also like' (Glycerin if viewing Ghost). Zero recommendations on PDP today.

case_studies.why: Direct athletic footwear/apparel peer. Under Armour had the same SFCC-adjacent keyword-search limitations. +35% conversion rate after Algolia deployment — the single most directly comparable benchmark for Brooks' search addressable revenue ROI calculation.

case_studies.result: +150% sales contribution from search, +37% conversion rate, -88% bounce rate
case_studies.why: Premium lifestyle brand expanding globally — mirrors Brooks' trajectory from specialty-running into lifestyle footwear at Paris Fashion Week. Lacoste replaced platform-native Solr-based search with Algolia and more than doubled global sales contribution from search.

case_studies.result: +4.5% conversion during first Cyber Weekend, team productivity doubled
case_studies.product: AI Search & Discovery + Merchandising Studio + Dynamic Re-Ranking + Analytics
case_studies.why: Specialty footwear retailer on composable commerce stack (analogous to Brooks' SFCC). Shoe Carnival's merchandising team can now configure promotional banners and re-ranking without engineering tickets — directly solving Brooks' inability to inject Cascadia Elite banners for the 'trail' query.

demos.today: Redirects to /en_us/featured/best-sellers/ — zero relevance to query
demos.with_algolia: NeuralSearch returns: Glycerin 22 (max cushion), Ghost 17 (neutral road), Adrenaline GTS 24 (support) — all tagged for long-distance road running

demos.today: 54 identical results regardless of browsing history
demos.with_algolia: Ghost family boosted based on Ghost 16 browse signal; preferred gender/cushioning surfaced first for logged-in Run Club member

demos.today: Same static facets as any query — Product Gender, Size, Product Type, Best For, Support Type, Surface
demos.with_algolia: Dynamic Facets: width options appear first (women's-specific), men's sizes hidden, 'Best For' re-ordered for women's running categories

Co-sell: engage Algolia's Salesforce alliance team to lead a joint SFCC cartridge demo for Brooks Running's digital team. Integration complexity rated LOW — estimated 2–3 week implementation.
